extra.reasoning:
This is a state machine. The safety property is globally(opted_out implies not sent): no reachable state may have opted_out=true and sent=true simultaneously.

The transition t_resume_glitch ([on send_attempt if (opted_out and ticks >= 12): sent := true]) directly sets sent=true while opted_out is true, so it is the candidate for a violation. To fire it we must reach opted_out=true with ticks>=12, then deliver send_attempt.

Counterexample trace (14 events, within the 16-event bound):
1. optout (t_optout, guard true): opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=0.
2-13. tick x12 (t_tick, guard true): ticks increments 0->12 (capped at 12), sent reset to false each time. After the 12th tick: opted_out=true, sent=false, ticks=12.
14. send_attempt: with opted_out=true and ticks>=12, t_resume_glitch is enabled and sets sent:=true. Resulting state: opted_out=true, sent=true, ticks=12.

This reachable state has opted_out=true and sent=true, violating the property.

Note on nondeterminism: on send_attempt with opted_out=true, both t_send_blocked (sent:=false) and t_resume_glitch (sent:=true, requires ticks>=12) are enabled. Since the spec gives no priority resolution and we are checking whether SOME reachable execution violates the property, the execution that selects t_resume_glitch is a valid reachable execution and constitutes the counterexample. The t_resume_glitch rule is the defect: it re-enables sending after opt-out once the tick counter saturates at 12.
